image_analysis.visible_text:
Internet Atheists @AtheistTakes · 7h
Thousands of years of philosophy have been defeated

r/atheism u/Beneficial-Yam-792 · 14h
If "God" can exist without a creator, we can also exist without a creator.

Religious people hate when I bring this up. They will adamantly say "Nothing can exist without a creator."

By their logic "God" would require a creator. Then they will say "Well God is the exception to our rule because everything comes from him."

Alright. By that logic we can also exist without a creator. You can remove the deity from the equation completely.

Why don't they see this?

249 upvotes, 41 comments, 23 shares
image_analysis.description: A Twitter screenshot showing a post from Internet Atheists account sharing a Reddit post from r/atheism. The Reddit post presents a logical argument about creation and existence, questioning religious logic about God not needing a creator.
image_analysis.actual_message: This is an atheist making what they believe is a clever logical argument against theistic beliefs about creation, essentially saying if God can exist without a creator, then everything else can too, making God unnecessary. The Twitter account is sarcastically claiming this defeats 'thousands of years of philosophy.'
image_analysis.hook: The oversimplified logic that ignores fundamental philosophical distinctions between contingent and necessary existence, plus the arrogant claim that 'thousands of years of philosophy have been defeated' by a basic Reddit post - this is prime material for pointing out the difference between actual philosophical argumentation and internet-level reasoning.

strategy_analysis.tone: confident and performative - celebrating a perceived intellectual victory over religious thought
strategy_analysis.assumptions: that this logical argument is novel and devastating to religious philosophy, when it's actually a well-known philosophical problem (infinite regress) that theologians have addressed for centuries
strategy_analysis.angle: disagree and provoke by showing this isn't the gotcha moment they think it is
strategy_analysis.why: Taking a nuanced philosophical stance that this is actually an ancient problem with sophisticated responses will trigger both atheists and religious people to engage defensively.

replies.reply: This argument was solved in the 13th century. Aquinas distinguished between contingent beings (requiring a cause) and necessary being (self-existent by nature). Reddit didn't defeat philosophy - it just discovered freshman-level questions.

This is like watching someone discover fire and claiming they invented heat.

The "first cause" problem has kept philosophers busy for millennia. Aquinas called it necessary vs contingent existence.

Reddit called it Tuesday.

The same logic that "defeats" God also defeats your own reasoning. If everything needs justification, then this argument needs justification, which needs justification... 

Watch: the question isn't whether God exists. It's whether the questioner does.

This isn't philosophy, it's freshman dorm room logic. The "gotcha" ignores that theologians distinguish between contingent existence (needs a cause) and necessary existence (exists by nature). Aquinas covered this 800 years ago. Reddit didn't just solve the cosmological argument.

Congratulations, you just discovered the cosmological argument that Aquinas addressed in the 13th century. Thousands of years of philosophy remain undefeated by a Reddit post that confused contingent existence with necessary existence. Philosophy requires more than copy-pastin...

Aquinas solved this 800 years ago. Contingent beings need creators. Necessary being doesn't. Your logic is like saying "If the foundation doesn't need support, why does the house?" 

Reddit atheism isn't philosophy. It's intellectual masturbation without the satisfaction.

This. The deeper issue: they think logic operates in a vacuum. Necessary vs contingent existence isn't some theological dodge - it's basic metaphysics. A Reddit post didn't just demolish Aquinas, Maimonides, and Avicenna. Philosophy requires precision, not just pattern matching.

This is like watching someone discover fire and claiming they've defeated the entire field of thermodynamics.

Aquinas called this the infinite regress problem 800 years ago. Reddit just found out.
